provenance.route_notes: Paper describes design and proof-of-concept testing of a novel 3D printed Franz-cell-like diffusion cell with UV imaging. Uses a commercial 5% w/w ibuprofen gel through a silicone membrane (not skin). IVRT study, not IVPT. Endpoint data appears in Figure 11 (UV imaging concentration-time curve) over 12 h at 37°C, n=3. No formal formulation composition table; formulation details are minimal (commercial product). Receptor fluid: phosphate buffer pH 7.2. Orifice area 3.14 cm2.

provenance.route_notes: Study compares ibuprofen permeation across human skin, porcine skin, silicone membrane, and PAMPA using Franz cells for skin/silicone studies. Finite dose conditions. Four formulations: IBUGEL (5% gel), IBULEVE (5% spray), PG solution (5%), PEG300 solution (5%). Timepoints: 6h for silicone/PAMPA, 48h for skin. Table 1 lists excipients. Data primarily in figures with numerical summaries in text and abstract.

Four formulations: IBUGEL (5% gel), IBULEVE (5% spray), PG solution (5%), PEG300 solution (5%). Timepoints: 6h for silicone/PAMPA, 48h for skin. Table 1 lists excipients. Data primarily in figures with numerical summaries in text and abstract.

provenance.route_notes: Study uses Franz cell assays with Strat-M synthetic membranes. This is an IVRT study focused on photo-switchable azobenzene ion-pairs of ibuprofen (P+-Ibuprofen). Endpoints reported as permeation curves in Figure 4C with time to equilibrium noted. No standard dermal formulation (cream/gel); the formulation is an 8 mM aqueous solution of the ion-pair. Franz cell mentioned only in Author Contributions, not described in detail in main text.

evidence_items.snippet: 36, 48, 72 h. Formulation details in Table I (nanosuspension characterization) and Table II (DOE factorial design with flux and cumulative permeation). Key variables: particle size, vitamin E TPGS concentration, HPMC K100 concentration. Flux units: µg/cm²/h. dermatomed porcine skin; human skin skin

website of the United States government Here's how you know Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official governme Study uses ibuprofen as a model drug in nanosuspension gel formulations. ; accepted 12 July 2019; published online 24 July 2019 Abstract. The humidity was a well-known method to hydrate the skin; however, the published data were varied, and systemic experiments in the previous papers were few. Therefore, the in vitro permeation of excised porcine ear skin by drugs with different polarities [aminopyrine (AMP), antipyrine (ANP), methylparaben (MP), and ibuprofen (IP)] was analyzed under a constant skin surface temperature with different temperatures and humidities to reveal the effects of

ed 12 July 2019; published online 24 July 2019 Abstract. The humidity was a well-known method to hydrate the skin; however, the published data were varied, and systemic experiments in the previous papers were few. Therefore, the in vitro permeation of excised porcine ear skin by drugs with different polarities [aminopyrine (AMP), antipyrine (ANP), methylparaben (MP), and ibuprofen (IP)] was analyzed under a constant skin surface temperature with different temperatures and humidities to reveal the effects of tempera

increasing the relative humidity (RH) from 28 to 70% at 27°C led to a signiﬁcant increase in + percutaneous absorption of caffeine (8). These analyses were performed using devices that control humidity and skin surface temperature on the donor side while the receptor ﬂuid temperature was maintained at 37°C. Ishida et al. used a CO2 chamber to show that the effect of humidity on the in vitro penetration of human skin by drugs increased the permeability coefﬁcient (P) values of nicotine and isosorbide dinitrate at 9

solution for each model drug was stirred for 1-h at 40°C or 27°C according to the skin surface temperature before conducting the permeation experiment. The diffusion cell was equilibrated for 1-h for each environmental condi- tion, followed by the addition of 1.0 ml of saturated donor solution to each donor cell. During the permeation study, 100 μl of receiver ﬂuid was withdrawn every 1-h, and an equal volume of fresh PBS was added immediately. The receiver samples were analyzed using an HPLC system, as described b

provenance.route_notes: Ibuprofen (IP, log Ko/w 3.94) is one of four model drugs studied. IVPT using Franz diffusion cells with excised porcine ear skin. Study focuses on temperature and absolute humidity effects on skin permeation. Saturated donor solutions used. 8-hour permeation with 1-hour acclimation. Skin surface temperature controlled at 40°C or 29°C. Permeation parameters (Jss, P, tlag, KL, DL-2, ER) reported.

provenance.route_notes: Two-chamber horizontal diffusion cell, not Franz cell. IVRT study using silicone membrane as skin model. Ibuprofen used as anionic component of ionic liquid (lidocaine-ibuprofen IL). Steady-state flux (fluxss) reported in µmol/cm2/h from 1–8 h. No skin (human or animal) used.

route_anchor_evidence.locator: Page 3, Calculation of Steady-State Flux section and Results (Figs 3–4): fluxss calculated from cumulative permeation slope from 1 to 8 h; values reported as µmol/cm²/h
route_anchor_evidence.snippet: dQ/dtIL(Av = 1): lidocaine and ibuprofen permeation rates from IL preparation #5 were 3.99 ± 0.06 and 1.18 ± 0.05 µmol/cm2/h, respectively; dQ/dtsol(Av = 1): lidocaine and ibuprofen permeation rates from suspended aqueous solution were 7.92 ± 0.39 and 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm2/h

LBG (cp = 0.5% w/V) was dispersed in distilled water, under magnetic and mechanical stirring, at 80° C for 24 h and at room temperature for 24 additional hours (37). The solutions were then exhaustively dialyzed at 4° C against distilled water, using dialysis membranes with a cut-off 12,000–14,000. In order to convert the xanth polymer into the sodium form, NaOH 0.2 N was added to the dialyzed solution up to pH = 7.0. Finally, the samples were freeze-dried and stored in a desiccator until use. le preparation) x 100 (1) Preparation of the LBG/Xanthan hydrogel loaded with the vesicles. The LBG/Xanthan hydrogels were prepared by mixing appropriate amounts of LBG and xanth solutions (cp = 0.5 % w/V) previously autoclaved at 121° C for 20 min. Aliquots (4 mL) of each polymer solution were mixed for 15 min at 60° C (T>Tmelting of the Xanth chains) in the presence of 2 cm3 of the vesicle solutions in order to obtain a homogeneous system. provenance.route_notes: This is an IVRT study using a beaker setup (not a Franz diffusion cell) with no membrane barrier; the gel itself is the matrix. IBU is used as a model drug in niosome-loaded LBG/xanthan hydrogel. Release data reported in figures with regression equations; ~60% IBU released at 48h. No skin or synthetic membrane barrier used.

LBG (cp = 0.5% w/V) was dispersed in distilled water, under magnetic and mechanical stirring, at 80° C for 24 h and at room temperature for 24 additional hours (37). The solutions were then exhaustively dialyzed at 4° C against distilled water, using dialysis membranes with a cut-off 12,000–14,000. In order to convert the xanth polymer into the sodium form, NaOH 0.2 N was added to the dialyzed solution up to pH = 7.0. Finally, the samples were freeze-dried and stored in a desiccator until use. source_notes: This is an in vivo dermatopharmacokinetic (DPK) tape-strip study, not a classical Franz-cell IVPT/IVRT. No receptor medium or diffusion cell is used. Barrier is human stratum corneum in vivo. Endpoint is SC drug content (µg/cm²) at various time points post-formulation removal (clearance phase). Diffusion area estimated from template diameter of 2.5 cm (π×1.25²≈4.91 cm²). Initial uptake amount at time=0 post-removal from Figure 3 text is 12.2 ± 3.5 µg/cm²; Table II reports 13.6 ± 4.1 for delay=0. Both values are from separate subject sets but same protocol.

evidence_items.snippet: In vitro experiments using Franz cells, which were selected to follow the SC barrier function capability, demonstrated that Ac-treated skin retained caffeine and ibuprofen on the SC with very low permeation of both compounds into the deeper skin layers.

Full paper is required for quantitative extraction.

provenance.route_notes: IVPT study using Franz diffusion cells with porcine skin. Ibuprofen salts with L-valine alkyl esters (ethyl through hexyl) tested at 0.01 g/cm3 in 70% ethanol donor. Endpoints: cumulative permeated mass (mg/cm2), flux (mg/cm2/h), skin accumulation (mg/g skin), percentage of applied dose. Sampling at 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 24h. Numerical results likely in figures/tables not fully captured in supplied pages. ESI supplement referenced for DSC curves.

route_anchor_evidence.locator: Page 5, Skin permeation studies section: cumulative mass (mg/cm2) calculated at 0.5h, 1h, 2h, 3h, 4h, 5h, 8h, 24h; flux (mg/cm2/h) determined as slope of cumulative mass vs time; also skin accumulation (mg/g skin) after 24h.
route_anchor_evidence.snippet: The cumulative mass (mg cm2) was calculated based on this concentration. The flux (in mg cm2 h1) of the active drug – ibuprofen and its derivatives through the skin into acceptor fluid was determined as the slope of the plot of cumulative mass in the acceptor fluid versus time.

route_anchor_evidence.snippet: The commercial formulations selected for evaluation were IBUGEL™ (Ibuprofen 5% w/w) and IBULEVE™ Speed Relief 5% Spray. Two other formulations of ibuprofen were prepared as 5% w/w solutions in isopropyl alcohol and either PEG 300 or PG.

route_raw_labels.barrier_name_raw: human skin (heat-separated epidermis), full-thickness porcine ear skin, silicone membrane (250 µm), Skin PAMPA membrane
route_raw_labels.where_endpoint: Page 7 (Figure 1 and surrounding text): cumulative amounts permeated at 6h (~140 µg/cm2 silicone/PAMPA), 48h human skin 11-38 µg/cm2, porcine skin 59-81 µg/cm2; also Abstract page 2
route_raw_labels.endpoint_carrier_snippet: Figure 1 Cumulative amounts of ibuprofen permeated from IBUGEL®, IBULEVE®, PG and PEG 300 for: Human skin (A), Porcine skin (B), Silicone membrane (C) and Skin PAMPA dosed at 1 µl/cm2 (D). Each data point represents the mean±SD (n≥5).
route_raw_labels.formulation_carrier_snippet: The commercial formulations selected for evaluation were IBUGEL™ (Ibuprofen 5% w/w) and IBULEVE™ Speed Relief 5% Spray. Two other formulations of ibuprofen were prepared as 5% w/w solutions in isopropyl alcohol and either PEG 300 or PG.
route_raw_labels.notes: Study compares ibuprofen permeation across human skin, porcine skin, silicone membrane, and PAMPA using Franz cells for skin/silicone studies. Finite dose conditions. Four formulations: IBUGEL (5% gel), IBULEVE (5% spray), PG solution (5%), PEG300 solution (5%). Timepoints: 6h for silicone/PAMPA, 48h for skin. Table 1 lists excipients. Data primarily in figures with numerical summaries in text and abstract.

source_notes: Simple solution; endpoint value not individually tabulated in extracted pages; range across all formulations 11–38 µg/cm² at 48 h in human skin.
Figure endpoint built from triage + CV digitization + curve mapping.
